title: Rohitpandey08/automated-data-cleaning-pipeline:README.md
text: A production-style Python automation pipeline that cleans raw Excel and CSV files and generates analysis-ready datasets with schema validation. This project focuses on **automation, data quality, and real-world workflows**, moving beyond notebooks into reusable CLI-based data processing. ---

title: Rohitpandey08/automated-data-cleaning-pipeline:README.md
text: - Supports **Excel (.xlsx)** and **CSV (.csv)** files - Folder-based automation (no file renaming required) - Column name normalization (case, spaces, formatting) - Column alias mapping to handle inconsistent schemas - Required column validation (fail-fast mechanism) - Removes duplicate records and empty rows - Standardizes text, numeric, and date fields - Generates clean output files without modifying raw data ---

The solution simulates an **enterprise-grade ETL + BI workflow**, commonly used by: - Digital marketing teams - Growth & performance analysts - Business intelligence units From **raw Excel files** to **Python-based preprocessing**, **normalized MySQL modeling**, and **Power BI dashboards**, this project demonstrates strong fundamentals in **data engineering, analytics, and visualization**. --- -

🎯 Business Problem

Marketing teams often struggle to answer questions such as: - Which keywords generate the most traffic at the lowest cost? - Are we overspending on high-CPC, low-return keywords? - How does keyword difficulty affect traffic contribution? - What are the monthly and seasonal traffic trends? - Which keywords should be scaled, optimized, or paused? This project addresses these challenges by building a **structured analytics framework** for Google AdWords performance evaluation.

title: hcibikci/Python-Automation-Fatigue-Data-Analysis:README.md
text: In structural durability projects (both Automotive & Aerospace), engineers often deal with "Rainflow Counting" data containing millions of load cycles. Processing these datasets in **Excel** creates significant risks: * **Human Error:** Manual cell referencing mistakes. * **Performance:** Files crashing when exceeding 500MB. * **Lack of Traceability:** Hard to version control Excel formulas.

title: hcibikci/Python-Automation-Fatigue-Data-Analysis:README.md
text: I developed this Python script to automate the cumulative damage calculation. This approach treats engineering data as a software object, allowing for: * **Speed:** Reduces processing time from hours to seconds. * **Accuracy:** Vectorized calculations prevent manual errors. * **Scalability:** Can handle millions of rows without crashing.

title: hcibikci/Python-Automation-Fatigue-Data-Analysis:README.md
text: The fatigue life ($N_f$) is calculated using the log-log S-N relationship: $$\sigma_a = \sigma_f' (2N_f)^b$$ Where: * $\sigma_a$: Stress Amplitude * $\sigma_f'$: Fatigue Strength Coefficient * $b$: Fatigue Strength Exponent Cumulative Damage ($D$) is then derived: $$D = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \frac{n_i}{N_i}$$ If $D \ge 1.0$, failure is predicted. ---

title: VuppalaYashwanth/Python-Automation-Tool-for-CSV-Validation-Cleaning-Report-Generation:README.md
text: This project is a Python-based automation tool designed to improve the reliability of recurring CSV and spreadsheet-based reporting workflows. It validates dataset structure, cleans inconsistent data, and produces standardized outputs along with summary reports.

title: xavctn/img2table:README.md
text: `img2table` is a simple, easy to use, table identification and extraction Python Library based on [OpenCV](https://opencv.org/) image processing that supports most common image file formats as well as PDF files. Thanks to its design, it provides a practical and lighter alternative to Neural Networks based solutions, especially for usage on CPU.

title: xavctn/img2table:README.md
text: * [Installation](#installation) * [Features](#features) * [Supported file formats](#supported-file-formats) * [Usage](#usage) * [Documents](#documents) * [Images](#images-doc) * [PDF](#pdf-doc) * [Supported OCRs](#ocr) * [Table extraction](#table-extract) * [Excel export](#xlsx) * [Examples](#examples) * [Caveats / FYI](#fyi)

title: xavctn/img2table:README.md
text:
The library can be installed via pip: > pip install img2table: Standard installation, supporting Tesseract
> pip install img2table[paddle]: For usage with Paddle OCR
> pip install img2table[easyocr]: For usage with EasyOCR
> pip install img2table[surya]: For usage with Surya OCR
> pip install img2table[gcp]: For usage with Google Vision OCR
> pip install img2table[aws]: For usage with AWS Textract OCR
> pip install img2table[azure]: For usage with Azure Cognitive Services OCR

title: xavctn/img2table:README.md
text: * Table identification for images and PDF files, including bounding boxes at the table cell level * Handling of complex table structures such as merged cells * Handling of implicit content - see [example](/examples/Implicit.ipynb) * Table content extraction by providing support for OCR services / tools * Extracted tables are returned as a simple object, including a Pandas DataFrame representation * Export extracted tables to an Excel file, preserving their original structure

title: xavctn/img2table:README.md
text:
Images are loaded using the `opencv-python` library, supported formats are listed below.
Supported image formats
  • Windows bitmaps - .bmp, .dib
  • JPEG files - .jpeg, .jpg, *.jpe
  • JPEG 2000 files - *.jp2
  • Portable Network Graphics - *.png
  • WebP - *.webp
  • Portable image format - .pbm, .pgm, .ppm .pxm, *.pnm
  • PFM files - *.pfm
  • Sun rasters - .sr, .ras
  • TIFF files - .tiff, .tif
  • OpenEXR Image files - *.exr
  • Radiance HDR - .hdr, .pic
  • Raster and Vector geospatial data supported by GDAL
    OpenCV: Image file reading and writing
Multi-page images are not supported. ---

title: xavctn/img2table:README.md
text:
Images are instantiated as follows : >

Parameters

>
>
src : str, pathlib.Path, bytes or io.BytesIO, required
>
Image source
>
detect_rotation : bool, optional, default False
>
Detect and correct skew/rotation of the image
>

The implemented method to handle skewed/rotated images supports skew angles up to 45° and is based on the publication by Huang, 2020.
Setting the detect_rotation parameter to True, image coordinates and bounding boxes returned by other methods might not correspond to the original image.

title: xavctn/img2table:README.md
text:
PDF files are instantiated as follows : >

Parameters

>
>
src : str, pathlib.Path, bytes or io.BytesIO, required
>
PDF source
>
pages : list, optional, default None
>
List of PDF page indexes to be processed. If None, all pages are processed
>
detect_rotation : bool, optional, default False
>
Detect and correct skew/rotation of extracted images from the PDF
>
pdf_text_extraction : bool, optional, default True
>
Extract text from the PDF file for native PDFs
>
PDF pages are converted to images with a 200 DPI for table identification. ---

`img2table` provides an interface for several OCR services and tools in order to parse table content.
If possible (i.e for native PDF), PDF text will be extracted directly from the file and the OCR service/tool will not be called.

Images

PDF

title: xavctn/img2table:README.md
text:
Several Jupyter notebooks with examples are available :
  • Basic usage: generic library usage, including examples with images, PDF and OCRs
  • Borderless tables: specific examples dedicated to the extraction of borderless tables
  • Implicit content: illustrated effect of the parameter implicit_rows/implicit_columns of the extract_tables method

title: xavctn/img2table:README.md
text:
• For table extraction, results are highly dependent on OCR quality. By design, tables where no OCR data can be found are not returned.
  • The library is tailored for usage on documents with white/light background. Effectiveness can not be guaranteed on other type of documents.
  • Table detection using only OpenCV processing can have some limitations. If the library fails to detect tables, you may check CNN based solutions.

text: ![Python Version](https://img.shields.io/badge/python-3.6+-blue.svg) ![license](https://img.shields.io/badge/license-MIT-green.svg) Transform your scaned PDFs into actionable data with our advanced PDF Table Extractor. Utilizing state-of-the-art OCR and AI techniques, this Python tool effortlessly converts PDF documents into editable text formats, identifies and extracts tables, and integrates with Hugging Face Hub for further text processing.

title: Baskar-forever/TableExtractor-Advanced-PDF-Table-Extraction:README.md

title: Baskar-forever/TableExtractor-Advanced-PDF-Table-Extraction:README.md
text: - **PDF to Image Conversion:** Transforms PDF pages into images, preparing them for table detection and extraction. - **Advanced Table Detection:** Employs morphological transformations to detect tables within images. - **OCR Text Extraction:** Leverages OCR technology to extract text from tables accurately. - **AI-Powered Text Processing:** Cleans and formats extracted text, using AI models from Hugging Face Hub. - **Structured Data Output:** Aggregates extracted data into a structured and usable format.

title: Baskar-forever/TableExtractor-Advanced-PDF-Table-Extraction:README.md
text: Ensure you have the following prerequisites installed on your machine: - Python 3.6 or later - OpenCV (`cv2`) for image processing - NumPy for array manipulation - PyTesseract for OCR capabilities - pdf2image for converting PDF pages into images - PIL (Python Imaging Library) for image operations - Hugging Face Transformers for AI model integration

title: JuniverseCoder/MinerU2PPT:docs/core-flow/ocr-bbox-xy-refine-flow.md
text: - Flow name: OCR text bbox refinement in `refine_ocr_text_elements` - Trigger: OCR text elements are extracted and line fragments are merged - Entry/exit points: - Entry: `converter/ocr_merge.py::refine_ocr_text_elements` - Exit: refined OCR text elements with updated `bbox`, `lines[].bbox`, `spans[].bbox` - OCR detection parameters: - Optional DB detection settings (det_db_thresh / det_db_box_thresh / det_db_unclip_ratio) are passed when the OCR engine is initialized and influence upstream detection before refinement.

title: JuniverseCoder/MinerU2PPT:docs/core-flow/ocr-bbox-xy-refine-flow.md
text: 1. Convert element/line bbox from JSON coordinates to pixel coordinates. 2. Expand bbox to a padded window (`_expand_bbox_with_pad`). 3. Estimate `bg_color` and `font_color` from original bbox. 4. Build row and column hit flags in the padded window: - rows: `_build_row_font_flags` - cols: `_build_col_font_flags` 5. Refine Y boundaries (top/bottom) with two-stage logic: - first-wave trim inside original box, - second-wave extend on untouched side, - boundary alignment by row flags. 6. Refine X boundaries (left/right) with the same two-stage logic: - first-wave trim inside original box, - second-wave extend on untouched side, - boundary alignment by column flags. 7. Clamp refined bbox to image boundaries; if invalid, fallback to original bbox. 8. Convert refined pixel bboxes back to JSON coordinates. 9. Synchronize output geometry: - `line.bbox` uses refined line bbox, - `span.bbox` is aligned to refined line bbox in both X and Y, - final element bbox is union of refined lines. 10. Preserve debug fields: `ocr_bbox_original`, `ocr_bbox_pad`, `ocr_bbox_refined`.

title: Tanguy9862/Medical-OCR-Data-Extraction:README.md
text: Automated the extraction of medical data from scanned documents using **Optical Character Recognition (OCR)** and regular expressions, enabling efficient data processing and analysis.

title: Tanguy9862/Medical-OCR-Data-Extraction:README.md
text: This project focuses on automating the extraction of medical data from scanned documents. By leveraging OCR technology, the script reads text from images and applies regular expressions to extract specific data fields. It is designed to handle various types of medical reports, such as IPS (Intravascular Pressure System) and EFR (Electrocardiogram Frequency Response).

title: Tanguy9862/Medical-OCR-Data-Extraction:README.md
text: - **Language Variability**: One of the challenges was dealing with documents in multiple languages. The solution was to use langdetect to identify the language and then apply the appropriate regular expressions. - **Complex Data Fields**: Some medical data fields required intricate regular expressions for accurate extraction. This required a deep understanding of both medical terminologies and regular expression capabilities. - **Data Quality**: Scanned documents can have varying quality, affecting OCR performance. The project allows for custom Tesseract configurations to handle such cases.

title: Tanguy9862/Medical-OCR-Data-Extraction:README.md
text: This project involved regular collaboration with medical professionals to identify and prioritize critical data fields relevant for medical diagnoses and treatment plans. Their expertise guided the selection of key metrics to focus on during data extraction and analysis. A comprehensive RMarkdown document was also developed to perform statistical tests on the extracted data, featuring interactive graphs powered by Plotly to aid decision-making. Due to confidentiality, the RMarkdown file is not included in this repository.

title: bettercallmanav/resume-builder-skill:README.md
text: An AI skill that builds professional, high-impact resumes through a structured career consulting conversation. It doesn't just format text into a document — it conducts an honest review process that extracts real achievements, challenges weak claims, and produces a resume that tells a coherent career story.

title: bettercallmanav/resume-builder-skill:README.md
text: The skill walks through a 5-phase process: 1. **Context Gathering** — Understands the user's career level, target role, compensation goals, and current situation. Reads existing resumes and gives an honest initial assessment. 2. **Career Consulting** — Identifies core differentiators, assesses market value, and arrives at a clear positioning statement before any writing begins. 3. **Section-by-Section Review** — Goes through each resume section with brutal honesty. Extracts real metrics through probing questions. Never fabricates numbers. 4. **Document Generation** — Produces a polished `.docx` and `.pdf` with clickable links, bold metrics, right-aligned dates, and proper formatting using the `docx` npm package. 5. **Final Review** — Self-reviews the output for coherence, contradictions, and positioning alignment. Works for freshers, mid-career professionals, senior leaders, and career switchers across US, India, and European markets.

title: bettercallmanav/resume-builder-skill:README.md
text: - **Honest, not nice.** Weak content gets called out directly. - **Metrics must be real.** Every number is a promise the user will defend in an interview. - **Marketing document, not job description.** Outcomes over responsibilities. - **Positioning over completeness.** A focused narrative beats a comprehensive dump. - **One page first, two pages if earned.** Every line on page two must make the hiring manager more interested.

title: bettercallmanav/resume-builder-skill:resume-builder/SKILL.md
text: This skill builds professional resumes through an interactive consulting conversation. It does NOT just format text into a document. It conducts a structured review process that extracts real achievements, challenges weak claims, and produces a resume that tells a coherent career story. The process mirrors how a brutally honest career consultant would work: understand the person first, challenge their assumptions, extract real metrics, then build the document.

title: bettercallmanav/resume-builder-skill:resume-builder/SKILL.md
text: Read the following reference files based on the stage of the conversation: | Stage | Reference File | |-------|---------------| | Understanding user context | `references/career-consulting.md` | | Section-by-section writing | `references/section-framework.md` | | Generating the document | `references/docx-generator.md` | Also read the docx skill at `/mnt/skills/public/docx/SKILL.md` before generating any document output, as it contains critical formatting rules for .docx generation.

title: bettercallmanav/resume-builder-skill:resume-builder/SKILL.md
text: Start by understanding who you're working with. Ask these questions (adapt naturally, don't interrogate): 1. **Do you have an existing resume?** If yes, ask them to upload it. This is the starting point for review, not a blank slate build. 2. **Career level?** Fresher (0-1 years), early career (1-4 years), mid-career (5-10 years), senior (10+ years). This determines which sections matter and how aggressive the positioning should be. 3. **Target role?** What specific roles are they applying for? This shapes the entire narrative. A PM resume looks different from an engineering resume even for the same person. 4. **Target compensation?** Understanding their target helps calibrate how ambitious the positioning needs to be. Someone targeting 2x their current salary needs a fundamentally different resume than someone making a lateral move. 5. **Current situation?** Are they employed and exploring, actively job hunting, laid off, career switching? This affects urgency and strategy. If the user uploads an existing resume, read it carefully and perform an honest initial assessment before diving into sections. Call out what's strong and what's weak. Be specific, not vague.

title: bettercallmanav/resume-builder-skill:resume-builder/SKILL.md
text: Before writing a single word, understand the person's story. Read `references/career-consulting.md` for detailed guidance on: - How to assess their actual market value based on skills and experience - How to identify their core differentiator (the one thing that makes them different from 500 other applicants) - How to determine the right positioning strategy for their target role - How to handle career gaps, transitions, and non-linear paths - How to advise on what to include vs. exclude The goal is to arrive at a clear, one-sentence positioning statement before any writing begins. Examples: - "The PM who actually builds AI products" (technical PM) - "The engineer who thinks in business outcomes" (senior IC) - "Full-stack developer with production AI deployment experience" (mid-level dev) - "Computer science graduate with 3 published research papers" (fresher)

title: bettercallmanav/resume-builder-skill:resume-builder/SKILL.md
text: Go through each section one at a time. Read `references/section-framework.md` for the detailed framework for each section. The sections are modular. Not every resume needs every section. Here's the decision framework: | Section | Fresher | Early Career (1-4y) | Mid-Career (5-10y) | Senior (10y+) | |---------|---------|-------------------|-------------------|---------------| | Professional Summary | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | | Skills | Yes | Yes | Yes | Optional | | Experience | Internships | Primary focus | Primary focus | Primary focus | | Projects | Primary focus | If relevant | Only if exceptional | Rarely | | Education | Prominent | Standard | Compressed | One line | | Certifications | If relevant | If relevant | Only prestigious ones | Only prestigious ones | | Publications | If any | If relevant | If relevant | If relevant | | Open Source / GitHub | If any | If relevant | If relevant | If relevant | | School Marks (10th/12th) | Yes (India) | Yes (India) | Optional | No | For each section: 1. text: t version (if they uploaded a resume) 2. Give a brutal honest review 3. Ask probing questions to extract real metrics 4. Write the improved version 5. Compare old vs new explicitly 6. Get confirmation before moving to the next section **Critical rules during this phase:** - NEVER make up metrics. If you don't have a number, ask for it. If they don't have it, write the bullet without a number rather than fabricating one. - NEVER let vague claims pass. "Improved efficiency" means nothing. By how much? How measured? If they can't answer, rewrite without the claim. - Always ask: "Can you defend this in an interview?" If the answer is hesitation, the claim needs to go. - Challenge the user's attachment to weak content. Old internships, irrelevant projects, and buzzword skills sections should be questioned, but ultimately respect the user's decision after making your case.

title: bettercallmanav/resume-builder-skill:resume-builder/SKILL.md
text: Once all sections are reviewed and locked, generate the final document. Read `references/docx-generator.md` for the complete template code and formatting specifications. Key formatting requirements: - Single page for anyone under 10 years of experience (hard constraint) - Two pages maximum for 10+ years - All dates and locations right-aligned using tab stops - Key metrics bolded in experience bullets - Product names, company names, and links clickable (ExternalHyperlink) - Section headings with bottom border separator - Arial font throughout - Proper bullet points using LevelFormat.BULLET (never unicode bullets) - Output both .docx and .pdf After generating, preview the output and verify: - Fits on target page count - All links are clickable - Bold formatting renders correctly - Right-alignment is clean - No orphaned lines on a second page

title: bettercallmanav/resume-builder-skill:resume-builder/SKILL.md
text: After generating the document, do a final self-review: - Does the resume tell a coherent story from top to bottom? - Would a hiring manager understand the candidate's value in 6 seconds? - Are there any contradictions between sections? - Is the chronological order correct? - Do any metrics conflict or repeat? - Is the positioning statement reflected throughout? Share any issues found and fix them before delivering the final output.

title: bettercallmanav/resume-builder-skill:resume-builder/SKILL.md
text: **Be honest, not nice.** A weak resume with polite feedback helps nobody. Call out problems directly. Use phrases like "This is the weakest section," "This bullet says nothing," "A hiring manager would skip this." The user asked for help, not flattery. **Metrics must be real and defensible.** Every number on the resume is a promise the user will be asked to defend in an interview. One fabricated metric discovered kills credibility on all real metrics. When in doubt, leave it out. **The resume is a marketing document, not a job description.** It should answer "what did you achieve?" not "what were you responsible for?" Rewrite any bullet that describes duties rather than outcomes. **Positioning over completeness.** A focused resume that clearly communicates one strong narrative beats a comprehensive resume that tries to cover everything. Remove content that doesn't reinforce the core positioning, even if the user is attached to it. **Context matters.** Indian hiring norms differ from US norms. European CVs differ from American resumes. Academic CVs differ from industry resumes. Ask about the target geography and industry before applying universal rules.

title: bettercallmanav/resume-builder-skill:resume-builder/SKILL.md
text: differ from American resumes. Academic CVs differ from industry resumes. Ask about the target geography and industry before applying universal rules. **ATS and humans are both audiences.** The resume needs to pass automated parsing AND impress a human reader. Keywords from job descriptions should appear naturally in context, not dumped in a skills section. When there's tension between ATS optimization and readability, weave keywords into experience bullets rather than padding the skills section. **One page first, two pages if earned.** Always start with a one-page target. Tighten spacing, compress weak sections, and cut low-value content first. But if after honest optimization the user has genuinely strong content that doesn't fit (multiple products shipped, significant client work, relevant projects, publications, or certifications that strengthen positioning), expand to two pages rather than cutting achievements that help tell the story. The test is simple: does every line on page two make the hiring manager MORE interested, or is it filler to justify the second page? If it's filler, cut it. If it's genuinely strong, keep it.

title: bettercallmanav/resume-builder-skill:resume-builder/references/docx-generator.md
text: If the resume spills to two pages, first try to fit on one page: - Reduce top/bottom margins (minimum 540 DXA = 0.375 inches) - Reduce spacing between sections - Reduce bullet spacing to { before: 20, after: 20 } - As a last resort, reduce body font to size: 19 (9.5pt) - NEVER go below 9pt body text If after all tightening it still doesn't fit AND the content is genuinely strong (not filler), allow two pages. The test: does every line on page two make the hiring manager more interested? If yes, keep it. If no, cut until it fits on one page. For two-page resumes, ensure page breaks fall at natural section boundaries (not mid-bullet). The strongest content must be on page one.

text: Before writing the resume, understand where the person sits in the market. This determines how aggressive the positioning should be.

title: bettercallmanav/resume-builder-skill:resume-builder/references/career-consulting.md
text: - Built and shipped products with real users (not just prototypes or POCs) - Quantifiable business impact (revenue, cost savings, user growth) - Led teams or managed direct reports - Worked with recognizable companies or clients (even under NDA, categories like "Fortune 500 client" or "MBB consulting firm" carry weight) - Technical skills combined with business judgment (rare combination) - Multiple promotions in a short timeframe (signals internal validation) - Domain expertise in a hot market (AI, ML, security, fintech, etc.)

title: bettercallmanav/resume-builder-skill:resume-builder/references/career-consulting.md
section_title: Signals of Low Market Value (that the person may not realize)
text: - Long tenure at one company without title progression - Services/consulting company experience without product ownership - "Managed" or "coordinated" without building or delivering - Skills section full of buzzwords but no demonstrated application - Education-heavy resume with thin experience section - Side projects that were never shipped or have no users

title: bettercallmanav/resume-builder-skill:resume-builder/references/career-consulting.md
text: Ask directly: "What are you making now, and what are you targeting?" Then assess whether the gap is realistic. If someone earning 15 LPA wants 30 LPA, the resume alone won't close that gap. They need a positioning strategy, not just formatting. Factors that justify a significant salary jump: - Moving from services company to product company - Adding AI/ML skills with demonstrated application - Having shipped products with measurable business outcomes - Team leadership experience - Client-facing experience at recognizable brands

title: bettercallmanav/resume-builder-skill:resume-builder/references/career-consulting.md
text: Every resume needs a "so what?" answer. When a hiring manager reads it, they should be able to complete this sentence: "This person is interesting because ___."

title: bettercallmanav/resume-builder-skill:resume-builder/references/career-consulting.md
text: **Freshers:** Research publications, exceptional academic projects with real users, competitive programming rankings, open source contributions, standout internships at recognizable companies. **Early Career (1-4 years):** Shipped products with real metrics, rapid promotions, technical depth uncommon at their level (e.g., a PM who codes, an engineer who does customer research), cross-functional impact. **Mid-Career (5-10 years):** Domain expertise, team building track record, P&L ownership, system design at scale, strategic contributions beyond individual execution. **Senior (10+ years):** Organizational transformation, business unit creation, industry thought leadership, executive stakeholder management, board-level visibility.

title: bettercallmanav/resume-builder-skill:resume-builder/references/career-consulting.md
text: Ask the user: "If I showed your resume to a hiring manager alongside 50 others for the same role, what would make them put yours in the 'call' pile?" If they can't answer clearly, the resume doesn't have a strong enough differentiator. Work with them to find it before writing.

title: bettercallmanav/resume-builder-skill:resume-builder/references/career-consulting.md
text: The strongest positioning follows this pattern: "[Core Role] who [Unexpected Strength]" Examples: - "The PM who builds" (technical PM who codes) - "The engineer who closes deals" (technical sales / solutions engineer) - "The designer who ships" (design engineer) - "The analyst who automates" (data analyst with engineering skills) - "The marketer who measures" (growth / performance marketing) The second half should be surprising. If both halves are expected for the role, the positioning is too generic.

title: bettercallmanav/resume-builder-skill:resume-builder/references/career-consulting.md
text: - "Full-stack developer" (everyone says this) - "Passionate about technology" (means nothing) - "Results-oriented professional" (every resume says this) - "Team player with excellent communication skills" (filler) - Listing 15+ skills without depth in any of them

title: bettercallmanav/resume-builder-skill:resume-builder/references/career-consulting.md
text: Don't hide gaps. Hiring managers calculate dates. Instead: - If gap < 6 months: No explanation needed, normal between-jobs period. - If gap 6-12 months: Brief mention if asked, but resume shouldn't draw attention to it. - If gap > 12 months: Address it in the resume with what was done (freelancing, upskilling, personal projects, caregiving). Honesty beats creative date formatting.

title: bettercallmanav/resume-builder-skill:resume-builder/references/career-consulting.md
text: Career switches are increasingly common. Frame the switch as intentional, not accidental: - Highlight transferable skills from the old career - Show deliberate steps toward the new career (projects, courses, part-time work) - Lead the summary with the target role, not the origin role

title: bettercallmanav/resume-builder-skill:resume-builder/references/career-consulting.md
text: Many people from consulting or services companies struggle because: - Their work was for clients they can't name (NDA) - They don't "own" products - Metrics are hard to claim when the client owns the outcome Solutions: - Use category descriptors instead of names: "Fortune 500 retail client," "MBB consulting firm," "Big 4 bank" - Frame client work as product delivery: "Built and deployed an AI-powered analytics platform for a Fortune 500 client" - Focus on what YOU did, not what the client achieved afterward - Highlight internal tools or platforms you built for your own company

title: bettercallmanav/resume-builder-skill:resume-builder/references/career-consulting.md
text: Some people inflate everything. Others undersell everything. Both are problems. **Over-claiming signals:** - Every metric is a round number (30%, 40%, 50%) - Claims can't be explained with a clear methodology - Numbers conflict across sections - Achievements sound too good for the role level **Under-claiming signals:** - "Helped with" or "was part of" instead of "built" or "led" - Missing metrics that almost certainly exist (e.g., built a product but can't say how many users) - Burying the strongest achievements in the middle of a bullet - Leading with responsibilities instead of outcomes Push over-claimers to be honest. Push under-claimers to own their work.

title: bettercallmanav/resume-builder-skill:resume-builder/references/career-consulting.md
text: - 10th and 12th marks are commonly expected on application forms and sometimes parsed from resumes. Include them for anyone under 5 years of experience, compress to one line. - College internships carry more weight than in the US/EU. Keep them for anyone under 3 years of experience, but compress. - "Lakh Per Annum" (LPA) is the standard compensation unit. Understand the market ranges for different cities and roles. - Services companies (TCS, Infosys, Wipro, Cognizant) are seen differently from product companies. If someone is trying to transition, the resume needs to emphasize product thinking and ownership over project management. - Notice periods in India are typically 30-90 days. This affects job search timeline.

title: bettercallmanav/resume-builder-skill:resume-builder/references/career-consulting.md
text: - No photos, no personal details (age, marital status, nationality) - GPA only if > 3.5 and < 3 years out of school - One page strongly preferred for under 10 years - ATS optimization is critical; many large US companies use automated screening

title: bettercallmanav/resume-builder-skill:resume-builder/references/career-consulting.md
text: - "CV" is the standard term, not "resume" - Photos may be expected in some countries (Germany, France) but not others (UK) - Education section often includes more detail - Two pages is more acceptable even for early career

title: bettercallmanav/resume-builder-skill:resume-builder/references/section-framework.md
text: 1. Name & Contact (always first) 2. Professional Summary 3. Skills 4. Experience 5. Projects (if included) 6. Education 7. Certifications (if included) This order works for 90% of cases. Exceptions: freshers with no experience should lead with Education, then Projects, then Internships. Career switchers may lead with a custom "Relevant Experience" section.

title: bettercallmanav/resume-builder-skill:resume-builder/references/section-framework.md
text: - Name and title should be the same font size, both bold - Contact info on one line, centered, smaller font - Email and LinkedIn MUST be clickable hyperlinks - GitHub link only if the profile has meaningful activity (not an empty account) - Portfolio/website link only if it's live, maintained, and reinforces positioning - No physical address beyond city/country - No photo (unless required by regional norms)

title: bettercallmanav/resume-builder-skill:resume-builder/references/section-framework.md
text: - Using "Curriculum Vitae" or "Resume" as a header (unnecessary, everyone knows what this document is) - Including date of birth, marital status, or nationality (not needed in most markets) - Listing a personal website that's under construction or broken - Using a non-professional email address

title: bettercallmanav/resume-builder-skill:resume-builder/references/section-framework.md
text: Give the hiring manager the complete picture in 3-5 sentences. If they read nothing else, they should know: what you do, what makes you special, and the scale of your impact.

title: bettercallmanav/resume-builder-skill:resume-builder/references/section-framework.md
text: Sentence 1: [Role] with [X years] of experience [core competency]. Sentence 2: [Biggest achievement with specific metrics]. Sentence 3: [Second biggest achievement or scope indicator]. Sentence 4: [Credibility signal — promotions, notable clients, team leadership]. Sentence 5: [Technical skills if relevant to the role — "Hands-on with X, Y, Z"].

title: bettercallmanav/resume-builder-skill:resume-builder/references/section-framework.md
text: - Every sentence must contain a provable fact. No opinions, no adjectives. - Bold the key numbers: years of experience, user counts, customer counts, team size. - Do NOT include: "passionate about," "results-oriented," "team player," or any other filler. - The summary should be readable in under 10 seconds. - If the user's summary is longer than 5 sentences, it's too long.

title: bettercallmanav/resume-builder-skill:resume-builder/references/section-framework.md
text: - [ ] Does it answer "what does this person do?" in sentence 1? - [ ] Does it include at least one quantified achievement? - [ ] Is there a credibility signal (promotion, notable client, team size)? - [ ] Could this summary belong to 100 other people? (If yes, it's too generic) - [ ] Are there any adjectives that aren't backed by evidence?

title: bettercallmanav/resume-builder-skill:resume-builder/references/section-framework.md
text: Freshers don't have professional achievements. Lead with education, technical skills, and academic/personal projects: "Computer Science graduate from [University] with [GPA/distinction]. Built [project] that [outcome]. Technical skills in [X, Y, Z]."

title: bettercallmanav/resume-builder-skill:resume-builder/references/section-framework.md
text: Quick scan for hiring managers and ATS keyword matching. This is NOT the place to demonstrate expertise — that's what Experience is for.

title: bettercallmanav/resume-builder-skill:resume-builder/references/section-framework.md
text: Two categories maximum: - **Technical:** Languages, frameworks, tools, platforms actually used - **Product/Domain:** Methodologies, practices relevant to the target role Optional third line: - **Also worked with:** Lower-priority tools for ATS coverage

title: bettercallmanav/resume-builder-skill:resume-builder/references/section-framework.md
text: - Only list skills you can discuss intelligently in an interview - AI-assisted usage counts (e.g., using Next.js with Claude Code is still "Next.js" experience) - Never create subcategories like "Beginner/Intermediate/Advanced" — they invite doubt - Remove any skill that doesn't appear in job descriptions for your target role - Technical skills you actually use daily should come first in the list - Kill buzzword frameworks: RACI Matrix, Moscow Method, Product Vision Board, Design Thinking as standalone skills. These are methodologies that belong in context, not in a keyword list.

title: bettercallmanav/resume-builder-skill:resume-builder/references/section-framework.md
text: If keywords are removed from Skills, they must appear naturally in Experience bullets. ATS scans the entire document, not just the skills section. The resume's total keyword coverage matters, not where keywords appear.

title: bettercallmanav/resume-builder-skill:resume-builder/references/section-framework.md
text: Freshers can be more generous with skills since their Experience section is thin. Include academic tools, lab equipment, languages, and relevant coursework topics. But still avoid listing things you can't discuss.

title: bettercallmanav/resume-builder-skill:resume-builder/references/section-framework.md
text: **Option A: Single consolidated entry per company (recommended for most)** **Option B: Separate entries per role (use when roles were fundamentally different)** Use Option A when: Same company, same general function, promotions show progression but the work is a continuous story. Use Option B when: Roles were genuinely different (e.g., moved from engineering to product within the same company).

title: bettercallmanav/resume-builder-skill:resume-builder/references/section-framework.md
text: Examples: - "Built an internal vendor intelligence platform replacing manual workflows across 23 countries and 1,500+ vendors. Reduced evaluation time from 1-3 days to under 10 minutes." - "Led a 4-person engineering team delivering 4 AI POCs for an MBB consulting firm in collaboration with a major cloud provider." - "Redesigned the checkout flow for a B2C e-commerce platform, increasing conversion rate from 2.1% to 3.8%."

title: bettercallmanav/resume-builder-skill:resume-builder/references/section-framework.md
text: When the user doesn't have metrics, probe with: - "What was the process BEFORE you did this? How long did it take?" - "How many people use what you built?" - "How many [units of work] did you process/handle?" - "What was the team size you worked with?" - "Did this replace a manual process? What did that cost?" - "Can you compare before vs after in any measurable way?" If probing yields nothing concrete, write the bullet without a metric rather than fabricating one. A honest "Built X for Y team" is better than a fake "Improved Z by 40%."

title: bettercallmanav/resume-builder-skill:resume-builder/references/section-framework.md
text: Bold key metrics and achievement numbers to create visual anchors for scanning: - User/customer counts: **20+ enterprise customers**, **1,000+ users** - Time improvements: **1-2 weeks to under a day** - Scale indicators: **23 countries**, **1,500+ vendors** - Notable collaborations: **4 AI POCs for an MBB consulting firm** Do NOT bold everything. If more than 30% of text is bold, nothing stands out.

title: bettercallmanav/resume-builder-skill:resume-builder/references/section-framework.md
text: Make product names clickable when they have live URLs: - Company products the user built (if public) - Open source projects with GitHub repos - Published apps or websites - Portfolio pieces Do NOT link to: internal tools (obvious), broken/dead URLs, private repos.

title: bettercallmanav/resume-builder-skill:resume-builder/references/section-framework.md
text: Most recent role first, always. Within a role, most impactful bullet first, not chronological order of when things happened.

title: bettercallmanav/resume-builder-skill:resume-builder/references/section-framework.md
text: When the user can't name clients or projects: - Use category descriptors: "MBB consulting firm," "Fortune 500 retail client," "Big 4 bank" - Never name the specific company - Category should be specific enough to signal caliber but vague enough to protect identity - "A top-tier consulting firm" is weaker than "an MBB consulting firm" (3 companies vs hundreds)

title: bettercallmanav/resume-builder-skill:resume-builder/references/section-framework.md
text: - Bullets that describe duties rather than achievements ("Responsible for managing...") - Bullets that repeat achievements from other sections - Metrics that conflict with other metrics on the same resume - Anything the user can't confidently explain in an interview - Old roles that add no value to the current narrative (usually 8+ years old)

title: bettercallmanav/resume-builder-skill:resume-builder/references/section-framework.md
text: - Freshers: ALWAYS. This is your Experience substitute. - Early career: Only if projects reinforce your positioning AND are shipped/live. - Mid-career+: Only exceptional projects (open source with stars, products with real users).

title: bettercallmanav/resume-builder-skill:resume-builder/references/section-framework.md
text: - Only include projects that are shipped and accessible. "Built but never launched" is not a project; it's a learning exercise. - Include the tech stack for each project. - Include a clickable link (GitHub, live URL) when available. - If the project is dead/offline, don't include it.

title: bettercallmanav/resume-builder-skill:resume-builder/references/section-framework.md
text: - GPA only if above 7.5/10 or 3.5/4.0. Below that, omit it. - School marks: Include for Indian resumes under 5 years experience (application portals parse them). Compress to single lines. - No school names needed for 5+ years experience. - Certifications go in a separate section, not under Education.

title: bettercallmanav/resume-builder-skill:resume-builder/references/section-framework.md
text: - The certification is recognized in the industry (AWS, GCP, PMP, CFA) - It's relevant to the target role - It was earned recently (within 3 years)

title: bettercallmanav/resume-builder-skill:resume-builder/references/section-framework.md
text: - Udemy, Coursera, or similar online course completions (these are not certifications) - Expired certifications - Certifications irrelevant to the target role - If the resume is already at one strong page, certifications can justify a clean second page alongside other content

title: bettercallmanav/resume-builder-skill:resume-builder/references/section-framework.md
text: - Keep for anyone under 3 years of professional experience - Compress to 1-2 bullets maximum per internship - Remove for anyone over 5 years of experience - Only keep internships at recognizable companies or with relevant work - If the internship work is similar to current work, it's redundant and should be cut

title: bettercallmanav/resume-builder-skill:resume-builder/references/section-framework.md
text: - Always TARGET one page first. Tighten spacing, compress weak sections, and cut low-value content before expanding. - If after honest optimization the content is genuinely strong and doesn't fit, expand to two pages. Every line on page two must make the hiring manager more interested, not fill space. - Two pages of strong content beats one page with critical achievements cut. - Three pages maximum for 15+ years of experience. - Academic CVs: As many pages as needed.

title: bettercallmanav/resume-builder-skill:resume-builder/references/section-framework.md
text: - Arial or Calibri throughout. - Name: 16pt bold. - Section headings: 11pt bold, uppercase, with bottom border. - Body text: 10pt. - Contact info: 9pt.

title: bettercallmanav/resume-builder-skill:resume-builder/references/section-framework.md
text: - Tight but readable. No double spacing anywhere. - Consistent spacing between sections. - Bullet points with minimal spacing (30-40 DXA before/after).

title: bettercallmanav/resume-builder-skill:resume-builder/references/section-framework.md
text: - All dates and locations right-aligned using tab stops. - Content width = page width minus margins. - Tab stop position must match content width exactly for clean right-alignment.

title: bettercallmanav/resume-builder-skill:resume-builder/references/section-framework.md
text: - Use proper bullet formatting (LevelFormat.BULLET in docx-js), never unicode characters. - Each bullet should be 1-3 lines. Anything longer needs to be split or cut. - Start every bullet with a strong action verb: Built, Led, Designed, Delivered, Reduced, Increased, Launched, Architected, Managed, Shipped.

title: RayCarterLab/ExcelAlchemy:examples/README.md
text: These examples are organized as a recommended learning path rather than a flat list. If you want a single public-facing page that combines screenshots, representative workflows, and captured outputs, see [`docs/examples-showcase.md`](https://github.com/RayCarterLab/ExcelAlchemy/blob/main/docs/examples-showcase.md). If you want a copyable reference layout rather than a single script, see [`examples/fastapi_reference/`](https://github.com/RayCarterLab/ExcelAlchemy/blob/main/examples/fastapi_reference/README.md).

title: RayCarterLab/ExcelAlchemy:examples/README.md
text: 1. `annotated_schema.py` - Start here if you want to learn the declaration style first. - Shows the modern `Annotated[..., Field(...), ExcelMeta(...)]` pattern. - Best for: first-time readers who want to understand the schema declaration style. - Output: prints the generated template filename and the declared field labels. - Captured output: [`files/example-outputs/annotated-schema.txt`](https://github.com/RayCarterLab/ExcelAlchemy/blob/main/files/example-outputs/annotated-schema.txt) 2. `employee_import_workflow.py` - Read this next if you want to understand the core import story. - Shows template generation, workbook upload, import execution, and result reporting. - Best for: backend developers implementing the basic import path. - Output: prints success and failure counts, created row count, and uploaded result artifacts. - Captured output: [`files/example-outputs/employee-import-workflow.txt`](https://github.com/RayCarterLab/ExcelAlchemy/blob/main/files/example-outputs/employee-import-workflow.txt) 3. `create_or_update_import.py` - Read this after the basic import flow. - Shows `ImporterConfig.for_create_or_update(...)` with `is_data_exist`, `creator`, and `updater`.

text: import.py` - Read this after the basic import flow. - Shows `ImporterConfig.for_create_or_update(...)` with `is_data_exist`, `creator`, and `updater`. - Best for: admin tools and synchronization flows that mix creates and updates. - Output: prints created row count, updated row count, and the final import result. - Captured output: [`files/example-outputs/create-or-update-import.txt`](https://github.com/RayCarterLab/ExcelAlchemy/blob/main/files/example-outputs/create-or-update-import.txt) 4. `export_workflow.py` - Read this once the import flow is clear. - Shows artifact generation, export uploads, and a custom storage-backed export task. - Best for: download centers and reporting tasks that need workbook artifacts and upload URLs. - Output: prints artifact filename, byte size, upload URL, and uploaded object names. - Captured output: [`files/example-outputs/export-workflow.txt`](https://github.com/RayCarterLab/ExcelAlchemy/blob/main/files/example-outputs/export-workflow.txt) 5. `custom_storage.py` - Read this when you want to implement your own `ExcelStorage`. - Keeps the example minimal and focused on the protocol boundary.

title: RayCarterLab/ExcelAlchemy:examples/README.md
text: `custom_storage.py` - Read this when you want to implement your own `ExcelStorage`. - Keeps the example minimal and focused on the protocol boundary. - Best for: teams wiring ExcelAlchemy into their own object storage layer. - Output: prints the in-memory upload URL and uploaded byte count. - Captured output: [`files/example-outputs/custom-storage.txt`](https://github.com/RayCarterLab/ExcelAlchemy/blob/main/files/example-outputs/custom-storage.txt) 6. `date_and_range_fields.py` - Read this if you want to understand workbook-friendly date, date range, number range, and money fields. - Best for: data-entry templates with compensation, effective dates, or range fields. - Output: prints the generated template filename and the exported field labels. - Captured output: [`files/example-outputs/date-and-range-fields.txt`](https://github.com/RayCarterLab/ExcelAlchemy/blob/main/files/example-outputs/date-and-range-fields.txt) 7. `selection_fields.py` - Read this if your domain uses approval forms, assignments, ownership trees, or selection-heavy templates. - Best for: approval forms, personnel assignment forms, and selection-heavy business templates.

title: RayCarterLab/ExcelAlchemy:examples/README.md

For the reference-project version, use: If you want to smoke-test the web integration without running a server, execute:

title: RayCarterLab/ExcelAlchemy:examples/README.md
text: - The examples intentionally use in-memory storage so they stay self-contained. - They are meant to show the recommended public API shape for the stable 2.x line. - If you want a production backend, prefer `storage=...` with `MinioStorageGateway` or your own `ExcelStorage` implementation. - The built-in `minio_storage.py` example reflects the current 2.x Minio path, which still uses the compatibility configuration fields under the hood. - The smoke tests in `tests/integration/test_examples_smoke.py` cover the main example entry points directly. - Fixed example output assets are generated by `scripts/generate_example_output_assets.py`.

title: RayCarterLab/ExcelAlchemy:examples/fastapi_reference/README.md
text: This directory shows a minimal reference project structure for integrating ExcelAlchemy into a FastAPI service. If you want copyable success / failure response shapes that match this reference project, see [`docs/api-response-cookbook.md`](https://github.com/RayCarterLab/ExcelAlchemy/blob/main/docs/api-response-cookbook.md).

title: RayCarterLab/ExcelAlchemy:examples/fastapi_reference/README.md
text: - `models.py` Defines workbook schema declarations. - `schemas.py` Defines request schemas, payload models, and success/error response envelopes for the HTTP layer. - `responses.py` Builds stable import payloads from ExcelAlchemy result objects. - `presenters.py` Wraps payloads in HTTP-facing success/error envelopes. - `storage.py` Defines a request-scoped in-memory `ExcelStorage` implementation. - `services.py` Holds the import service layer and helper functions. - `app.py` Wires routes to the service layer and exposes a runnable FastAPI app.

title: RayCarterLab/ExcelAlchemy:examples/fastapi_reference/README.md
text: This is intentionally small, but it mirrors the shape of a real backend: - routes stay thin - workflow logic lives in a service - storage is injected instead of hard-coded - schema declarations stay separate from transport concerns

title: RayCarterLab/ExcelAlchemy:examples/fastapi_reference/README.md
text: - route layer and service layer separation - explicit request, payload, and error-response schemas - structured API response building with a stable envelope - injected storage rather than global singleton state - template download and workbook import endpoints - a small, copyable structure that can be adapted into a real backend project

title: RayCarterLab/ExcelAlchemy:examples/fastapi_reference/README.md
text: Downloads a workbook template generated from the importer schema. Response: - `200 OK` - `Content-Type: application/vnd.openxmlformats-officedocument.spreadsheetml.sheet` - `Content-Disposition: attachment; filename=employee-template.xlsx`

title: RayCarterLab/ExcelAlchemy:examples/fastapi_reference/README.md
text: Accepts an uploaded workbook and runs the import workflow. Request: - multipart form-data - file field name: `file` - optional form field: `tenant_id` Example: Example JSON response: If the workbook has validation errors, the same endpoint still returns a structured result payload. Application code can then read: - workbook-level result status from `result` - created row count from `created_rows` - uploaded result workbook names from `uploaded_artifacts` - cell-level frontend payloads from `cell_errors` - row-level frontend payloads from `row_errors` In the reference app, those values live under `data`, while `ok` tells the client whether the route returned a success or an API-layer error envelope. Example validation-error response shape: Example API-layer error response: In a real project, you would typically extend this response with: - request-scoped trace metadata - actor information - domain-specific identifiers or import job ids - your own error-code to UI-action mapping

title: RayCarterLab/ExcelAlchemy:docs/api-response-cookbook.md
text: For most backends, a stable import response can look like this: This shape works well because it separates: - workbook-level outcome from `result` - cell-level UI rendering from `cell_errors` - row-level summaries from `row_errors` Within each error item: - use `code` for machine-readable branching - use `message_key` when you want to map back to a localized message catalog - use `message` for logs or plain-text APIs - use `display_message` when you want ready-to-render UI text Developer diagnostics are intentionally separate from these payload fields. Application logs use named loggers such as `excelalchemy.codecs`, `excelalchemy.runtime`, and `excelalchemy.metadata`; API responses should rely on `code`, `message_key`, `message`, and `display_message` instead of raw log output.

title: RayCarterLab/ExcelAlchemy:docs/api-response-cookbook.md
text: Use this when the import completed without header or data failures. Frontend usage: - show a success toast from `result.result` - show imported row counts from `result.summary` - offer the result workbook download when `result.summary.result_workbook_url` is present

title: RayCarterLab/ExcelAlchemy:docs/api-response-cookbook.md
text: Use this when the header is valid but one or more rows failed validation. Frontend usage: - render a failed import banner from `result.is_data_invalid` - build a row-error table from `row_errors.summary.by_row` - build field filters or grouped panels from `cell_errors.summary.by_field` - branch on `code` for field-specific UI affordances

title: RayCarterLab/ExcelAlchemy:docs/api-response-cookbook.md
text: Use this when the workbook header row does not match the schema. Frontend usage: - show a header-level blocking dialog from `result.is_header_invalid` - render missing and unrecognized headers directly from `result.header_issues` - avoid showing row-level validation tables when there are no row errors

title: RayCarterLab/ExcelAlchemy:docs/api-response-cookbook.md
text: For larger backends, it is useful to wrap the three payload builders: This keeps your route layer thin and your API contract stable.

title: RayCarterLab/ExcelAlchemy:docs/api-response-cookbook.md
text: Common patterns: - use `result.result` for high-level status banners - use `result.summary.success_count` and `result.summary.fail_count` for summary chips - use `row_errors.summary.by_row` for row tables - use `cell_errors.items` for precise cell navigation - use `cell_errors.summary.by_code` for grouped issue badges - use `code` when you want machine-readable branching or localization on the frontend - use `message_key` when you maintain your own message catalog - use `message` when you want plain text without workbook decoration - use `display_message` when you want ready-to-render text

title: RayCarterLab/ExcelAlchemy:docs/locale.md
text: ExcelAlchemy currently distinguishes between three kinds of language output: - developer diagnostics, intended for logs and runtime troubleshooting - runtime messages, intended for Python developers and integrators - workbook display text, intended for spreadsheet users These layers do not currently share the same locale policy.

title: RayCarterLab/ExcelAlchemy:docs/locale.md
text: - Developer diagnostics are emitted through named loggers such as: - `excelalchemy.codecs` - `excelalchemy.runtime` - `excelalchemy.metadata` - Stability policy: diagnostics are intentionally standardized in English for the 2.x line - Intended audience: backend developers, operators, and maintainers Developer diagnostics are not the same surface as API payloads or workbook-facing messages. They are designed for logs, traces, and troubleshooting rather than end-user rendering.

title: RayCarterLab/ExcelAlchemy:docs/locale.md
text: - Supported runtime locale set: `('en',)` - Default runtime locale: `en` - Stability policy: runtime exceptions are intentionally standardized in English for the 2.x line This means error messages raised in Python code are expected to stay English unless the project explicitly announces broader runtime i18n support in a future release.

title: RayCarterLab/ExcelAlchemy:docs/locale.md
text: - Supported workbook display locales: `('zh-CN', 'en')` - Default workbook display locale: `zh-CN` - Stability policy: the default workbook locale is considered stable for the 2.x line Workbook display locale affects user-facing spreadsheet text such as: - import instructions in the first row - header comments - result and reason column labels - row validation status text - composite child labels such as start/end date and min/max value - workbook-facing boolean values such as `Yes/No` or `是/否`

title: RayCarterLab/ExcelAlchemy:docs/locale.md
text: - Developer diagnostics fall back to English - Runtime messages fall back to the runtime default locale: `en` - Workbook display messages fall back to the workbook display default locale: `zh-CN` If you pass an unsupported locale today, ExcelAlchemy will continue working and fall back to the default locale for that message layer.

title: RayCarterLab/ExcelAlchemy:docs/locale.md
text: Use `locale='zh-CN'` when the workbook is meant for Chinese-speaking spreadsheet users. Use `locale='en'` when the workbook is meant for English-speaking spreadsheet users. Examples:

title: RayCarterLab/ExcelAlchemy:docs/locale.md
text: - Constants in `excelalchemy.const` such as `HEADER_HINT`, `RESULT_COLUMN_LABEL`, and `REASON_COLUMN_LABEL` remain available as compatibility helpers and represent the stable `zh-CN` defaults. - Locale-aware behavior should be driven through `ImporterConfig(..., locale=...)` and `ExporterConfig(..., locale=...)`, not by reading those constants directly.

title: RayCarterLab/ExcelAlchemy:docs/locale.md
text: The i18n roadmap remains intentionally incremental: 1. keep developer diagnostics consistently English 2. keep runtime messages consistently English 3. keep workbook display locale explicit and stable 4. add new workbook locales additively 5. only expand runtime locale support when there is a clear maintenance plan

title: RayCarterLab/ExcelAlchemy:docs/public-api.md
text: This page summarizes which ExcelAlchemy modules are intended to be stable public entry points, which ones remain compatibility shims for the 2.x line, and which ones should be treated as internal implementation details. If you want the quickest path into the library, start with [`docs/getting-started.md`](https://github.com/RayCarterLab/ExcelAlchemy/blob/main/docs/getting-started.md). If you want a role-based reading path, see [`docs/integration-roadmap.md`](https://github.com/RayCarterLab/ExcelAlchemy/blob/main/docs/integration-roadmap.md). If you want concrete repository examples, see [`examples/README.md`](https://github.com/RayCarterLab/ExcelAlchemy/blob/main/examples/README.md) and [`docs/examples-showcase.md`](https://github.com/RayCarterLab/ExcelAlchemy/blob/main/docs/examples-showcase.md). If you want result-object guidance for backend or frontend integration, see [`docs/result-objects.md`](https://github.com/RayCarterLab/ExcelAlchemy/blob/main/docs/result-objects.md). If you want copyable backend response shapes, see [`docs/api-response-cookbook.md`](https://github.com/RayCarterLab/ExcelAlchemy/blob/main/docs/api-response-cookbook.md).

title: RayCarterLab/ExcelAlchemy:docs/public-api.md
text: These modules are the recommended import paths for application code: - `excelalchemy` The package root re-exports the most common public types, codecs, config objects, and result models. - `excelalchemy.config` Public workflow configuration objects such as `ImporterConfig`, `ExporterConfig`, and `ImportMode`. - `excelalchemy.metadata` Public metadata entry points such as `FieldMeta(...)`, `ExcelMeta(...)`, and `PatchFieldMeta`. - `excelalchemy.results` Structured import result models such as `ImportResult`, `ValidateResult`, and `ValidateHeaderResult`. - `excelalchemy.exceptions` Stable exception module for `ConfigError`, `ExcelCellError`, `ExcelRowError`, and `ProgrammaticError`. - `excelalchemy.codecs` Public codec namespace for built-in Excel field codecs.

title: RayCarterLab/ExcelAlchemy:docs/public-api.md
text: - `ExcelStorage` The recommended backend integration contract for workbook IO. - `storage=...` The recommended backend configuration pattern in the 2.x line. - `ExcelArtifact` The recommended return shape when you need bytes, base64, or data URLs. - import inspection names: Prefer `worksheet_table`, `header_table`, `cell_error_map`, and `row_error_map` when reading import-run state from the facade. - structured error access: Prefer `CellErrorMap` and `RowIssueMap` helpers such as `to_api_payload()` when you need frontend-friendly or API-friendly validation output. The stable helper set also includes `records()`, `summary_by_field()`, `summary_by_row()`, and `summary_by_code()` where applicable.

title: RayCarterLab/ExcelAlchemy:docs/public-api.md
text: These imports still work in the 2.x line, but should be treated as migration paths rather than long-term public module choices: - `excelalchemy.exc` Deprecated compatibility layer. Prefer `excelalchemy.exceptions`. - `excelalchemy.identity` Deprecated compatibility layer. Prefer imports from the package root. - `excelalchemy.header_models` Compatibility layer; application code should not depend on it. - `excelalchemy.types.*` Deprecated compatibility namespace retained for 2.x migrations. - `excelalchemy.util.convertor` Deprecated compatibility import. Prefer `excelalchemy.util.converter`.

title: RayCarterLab/ExcelAlchemy:docs/public-api.md
text: These modules may change without notice and should not be imported directly in application code: - `excelalchemy.core.*` - `excelalchemy.helper.*` - `excelalchemy.i18n.*` - `excelalchemy._primitives.*` The internals are intentionally allowed to evolve as the 2.x architecture continues to consolidate.

title: RayCarterLab/ExcelAlchemy:docs/public-api.md
text: Prefer imports like: For most application code, these are the recommended import paths: - `from excelalchemy import ...` Use this for the common public types, codecs, result models, and facade. - `from excelalchemy.config import ...` Use this when you need workflow configuration types such as `ExporterConfig` or `ImportMode`. - `from excelalchemy.exceptions import ...` Use this when you catch or surface library-level exceptions. - `from excelalchemy.metadata import ...` Use this if you want the dedicated metadata entry points directly. - `from excelalchemy.results import ...` Use this if you need result models or richer error-map helper types directly. If you are building API responses from import failures, the recommended public result helpers are: - `CellErrorMap.to_api_payload()` - `RowIssueMap.to_api_payload()` - `CellErrorMap.records()` - `RowIssueMap.records()` - `CellErrorMap.summary_by_field()` - `CellErrorMap.summary_by_row()` - `CellErrorMap.summary_by_code()` - `RowIssueMap.summary_by_row()` - `RowIssueMap.summary_by_code()` Avoid depending on implementation details such as:

title: RayCarterLab/ExcelAlchemy:docs/public-api.md
text: For the stable 2.x line, the recommended backend integration path is: The `storage` object should implement `ExcelStorage`. The older built-in Minio fields: - `minio=...` - `bucket_name=...` - `url_expires=...` still work in 2.x as compatibility paths, but they are no longer the recommended public API shape and now emit deprecation warnings. If you need concrete examples of the recommended storage path, see: - [`examples/custom_storage.py`](https://github.com/RayCarterLab/ExcelAlchemy/blob/main/examples/custom_storage.py) - [`examples/export_workflow.py`](https://github.com/RayCarterLab/ExcelAlchemy/blob/main/examples/export_workflow.py) - [`docs/getting-started.md`](https://github.com/RayCarterLab/ExcelAlchemy/blob/main/docs/getting-started.md)

title: RayCarterLab/ExcelAlchemy:docs/public-api.md
text: The 2.x line keeps compatibility shims to support migration, but the long-term direction is: - public API from `excelalchemy`, `excelalchemy.config`, `excelalchemy.metadata`, `excelalchemy.results`, `excelalchemy.exceptions`, and `excelalchemy.codecs` - backend integration through `ExcelStorage` - internal orchestration and helper modules treated as implementation details For import-run state naming, the long-term direction is also: - clear facade inspection names such as `worksheet_table`, `header_table`, `cell_error_map`, and `row_error_map` - older aliases such as `df`, `header_df`, `cell_errors`, and `row_errors` retained only as 2.x compatibility paths

title: RayCarterLab/ExcelAlchemy:docs/architecture.md
text: `src/excelalchemy/core/alchemy.py` - owns the user-facing workflow - coordinates import/export operations - keeps the top-level API compact

title: RayCarterLab/ExcelAlchemy:docs/architecture.md
text: `src/excelalchemy/core/schema.py` - extracts Excel-facing layout from models - expands composite fields - validates ordering assumptions

title: RayCarterLab/ExcelAlchemy:docs/architecture.md
text: `src/excelalchemy/core/headers.py` - parses simple and merged headers - validates workbook header rows against schema layout

title: RayCarterLab/ExcelAlchemy:docs/architecture.md
text: `src/excelalchemy/core/rows.py` - aggregates flattened worksheet rows back into model-shaped payloads - maps row/cell errors back into workbook coordinates

title: RayCarterLab/ExcelAlchemy:docs/architecture.md
text: `src/excelalchemy/core/executor.py` - validates row payloads - dispatches create/update/upsert logic - isolates backend execution from parsing concerns

title: RayCarterLab/ExcelAlchemy:docs/architecture.md
text: `src/excelalchemy/core/rendering.py` `src/excelalchemy/core/writer.py` - turns worksheet tables into workbook payloads - applies comments, colors, result columns, and workbook hint text

title: RayCarterLab/ExcelAlchemy:docs/architecture.md
text: `src/excelalchemy/core/storage_protocol.py` `src/excelalchemy/core/storage.py` `src/excelalchemy/core/storage_minio.py` - defines a stable storage contract - resolves configured storage strategy - ships one built-in Minio implementation

title: RayCarterLab/ExcelAlchemy:docs/architecture.md
text: `src/excelalchemy/metadata.py` - exposes `FieldMeta(...)` / `ExcelMeta(...)` as the stable public entry points - keeps `FieldMetaInfo` as a compatibility facade for the 2.x line - splits the real metadata state into declaration, runtime binding, workbook presentation, and import-constraint layers - keeps runtime metadata separate from validation backend internals

title: RayCarterLab/ExcelAlchemy:docs/architecture.md
text: `src/excelalchemy/helper/pydantic.py` - adapts Pydantic models to ExcelAlchemy needs - shields the rest of the codebase from version-specific framework details

title: RayCarterLab/ExcelAlchemy:docs/architecture.md
text: `src/excelalchemy/i18n/messages.py` - separates runtime errors from workbook display text - provides locale-aware workbook-facing messages

title: RayCarterLab/ExcelAlchemy:docs/architecture.md
text: Implement a new `ExcelFieldCodec` or `CompositeExcelFieldCodec` when you want custom workbook semantics. Built-in field annotations keep concise aliases like `Email` and `DateRange`, while the `*Codec` names expose the adapter role more explicitly.

title: RayCarterLab/ExcelAlchemy:docs/architecture.md
text: Both declaration styles are supported: - `FieldMeta(...)` as the concise compatibility-friendly syntax sugar - `Annotated[T, Field(...), ExcelMeta(...)]` as the more explicit Pydantic v2-first style

title: RayCarterLab/ExcelAlchemy:docs/architecture.md
text: - `src/excelalchemy/codecs/`: built-in Excel field codecs and codec base abstractions - `src/excelalchemy/metadata.py`: Excel-specific field metadata and declaration helpers - `src/excelalchemy/config.py`: importer/exporter configuration models - `src/excelalchemy/exceptions.py`: public exception types - `src/excelalchemy/_primitives/identity.py`: private typed string and index wrappers used across the core layer - `src/excelalchemy/_primitives/constants.py`: private constant and enum definitions - `src/excelalchemy/results.py`: import/export result models - `src/excelalchemy/_primitives/header_models.py`: private workbook header model objects - `src/excelalchemy/_primitives/deprecation.py`: private deprecation helpers used by compatibility shims - `src/excelalchemy/types/`: compatibility import layer for pre-refactor paths - `src/excelalchemy/exc.py`, `src/excelalchemy/identity.py`, `src/excelalchemy/header_models.py`, `src/excelalchemy/const.py`: compatibility or low-level facade modules kept at the package root Compatibility policy: - `excelalchemy.types.*` and `excelalchemy.types.value.*` remain available throughout the 2.x line - those imports emit `ExcelAlchemyDeprecationWarn

title: RayCarterLab/ExcelAlchemy:docs/architecture.md
text: : - `excelalchemy.types.*` and `excelalchemy.types.value.*` remain available throughout the 2.x line - those imports emit `ExcelAlchemyDeprecationWarning` at import time - the compatibility layer is scheduled for removal in ExcelAlchemy 3.0 - `excelalchemy.exc` now points to the public `excelalchemy.exceptions` module - `excelalchemy.identity` and `excelalchemy.header_models` remain as 2.x compatibility imports; prefer the package root or internal modules only

title: RayCarterLab/ExcelAlchemy:docs/architecture.md
text: The codebase is designed around stable seams: - facade vs collaborators - metadata vs validation backend - storage protocol vs concrete storage - workbook display text vs runtime messages Those seams are what made the later migrations possible without rewriting the whole project.

title: bassem-elsodany/mcp_excel_server:README.md
text: This project provides a set of tools for interacting with Excel workbooks and worksheets using the MCP (Message Control Protocol) server framework.

title: bassem-elsodany/mcp_excel_server:README.md
text: **MCP** stands for **Model Context Protocol**. It is an open protocol that standardizes how applications provide context to Large Language Models (LLMs). Think of MCP like a USB-C port for AI applications—it provides a standardized way to connect AI models to different data sources and tools. MCP helps you build agents and complex workflows on top of LLMs by: - Providing a growing list of pre-built integrations that your LLM can directly plug into - Offering flexibility to switch between LLM providers and vendors - Implementing best practices for securing your data within your infrastructure

title: bassem-elsodany/mcp_excel_server:README.md
text: The **MCP Server** is a lightweight program that exposes specific capabilities through the standardized Model Context Protocol. It securely accesses local data sources (like your computer's files and databases) and remote services (available over the internet) to provide context to LLMs.

title: bassem-elsodany/mcp_excel_server:README.md
text: The **MCP Client** is a protocol client that maintains a 1:1 connection with an MCP Server. It allows applications (like Claude Desktop, IDEs, or AI tools) to access data and tools provided by the server.

title: bassem-elsodany/mcp_excel_server:README.md
text: **MCP Tools** are specific capabilities exposed by MCP Servers. These tools enable LLMs to perform actions (e.g., creating a workbook, merging cells) and access data through a standardized interface. ---

title: bassem-elsodany/mcp_excel_server:README.md
text: ![MCP Excel Server](imgs/mcp_excel_server.jpg) **MCP Excel Server** is an implementation of the MCP Server that provides tools for automating Excel file operations using Python and [openpyxl](https://openpyxl.readthedocs.io/). It allows you to: - Create, open, and save Excel workbooks - Add, rename, copy, move, and delete worksheets - Read and write data to cells and ranges - Merge, unmerge, copy, move, and delete cell ranges - List available Excel files ---

If not provided, defaults to "Sheet1". - **Returns:** - `success`: True if the workbook was created successfully. - `message`: A message describing the result. - `info`: Additional information about the created workbook. - **[list_workbooks](src/mcp_excel_server/api/tools/workbook.py)**: List all Excel files in the configured directory. - **Args:** - None - **Returns:** - `success`: True if the operation succeeded. - `files`: List of Excel filenames. - `message`: Error message if success is False. - **[read_workbook_data](src/mcp_excel_server/api/tools/workbook.py)**: Read data from an Excel worksheet. - **Args:** - `filepath`: Name of the Excel file. - `sheet_name`: Name of the worksheet to read from. - `start_cell`: Starting cell reference (e.g., "A1"). If not provided, defaults to "A1". - `end_cell`: Ending cell reference (e.g., "B10"). If None, reads to the end of data. - `preview_only`: If True, returns only a preview of the data.

title: bassem-elsodany/mcp_excel_server:README.md
text: All MCP tools in this project are registered using the `@register_tool` decorator. This decorator is defined in [`src/mcp_excel_server/api/registry.py`](src/mcp_excel_server/api/registry.py) and serves the following purposes: - **Automatic Registration:** When you annotate a function with `@register_tool`, it is automatically registered as an MCP tool with the server. - **Name Prefixing:** The tool name is automatically prefixed with the value of `settings.tool_prefix` (by default, `"EXCEL_MCP_"`). For example, a function named `create_workbook` becomes a tool named `EXCEL_MCP_create_workbook`. - **Integration with FastMCP:** The decorator uses the MCP server's `.tool()` method to make the function available for remote invocation by AI agents or other clients. **Example Usage:** **How it works:** - The decorator constructs the tool name by concatenating the prefix and the function name. - It registers the function with the MCP server so it can be called as a tool. - The tool is then discoverable and invokable by any MCP-compatible client.

title: bassem-elsodany/mcp_excel_server:README.md

This isn't science fiction, it's happening right now, thanks to the Model Context Protocol (MCP) and my powerful Excel MCP Server.* ---

title: bassem-elsodany/mcp_excel_server:mcp_article.md
section_title: 🌟 What is MCP? The Universal Language for AI
text: Think of MCP as the **"USB-C for AI applications"** a standardized protocol that allows AI models to connect to any data source or tool with the same ease you plug in your phone charger. But what makes MCP truly revolutionary?

title: bassem-elsodany/mcp_excel_server:mcp_article.md
text: Before MCP, integrating AI with different applications was like trying to speak to someone who only understands a different language. Each AI model had its own way of connecting to databases, files, and tools. This created a fragmented ecosystem where: - 🔴 **Vendor Lock-in**: You were stuck with specific AI providers - 🔴 **Complex Integration**: Every new tool required custom development - 🔴 **Security Concerns**: Data had to be sent to external services - 🔴 **Limited Capabilities**: AI couldn't access your local resources

title: bassem-elsodany/mcp_excel_server:mcp_article.md
text: **How it works:** 1. **MCP Server** 🖥️ - A lightweight program that exposes specific capabilities - Lives on your machine or infrastructure - Securely accesses your data and tools - Think of it as your AI's personal assistant 2. **MCP Client** 🔌 - Connects to MCP servers (1:1 relationship) - Built into applications like Claude Desktop, IDEs, and AI tools - Acts as the bridge between AI and your tools 3. **MCP Tools** 🛠️ - Specific capabilities exposed by servers - Standardized interface for AI to perform actions - Can be anything: reading files, writing data, making API calls

title: bassem-elsodany/mcp_excel_server:mcp_article.md
text: - ✅ **Universal Compatibility**: Works with any MCP-compatible AI - ✅ **Local Processing**: Keep your data secure on your infrastructure - ✅ **Plug-and-Play**: Easy to add new capabilities - ✅ **Open Standard**: No vendor lock-in, community-driven ---

text: Excel Workflow Automation with OpenPyXL This Python script automates Excel spreadsheet processing using the openpyxl library. It modifies numerical values, adds a bar chart, and saves an updated version of the file. Features Reads an Excel file and processes data in Sheet1. Updates values in the third column (reducing by 10%) and writes them to the fourth column. Adds a bar chart to visualize the updated values. Saves a new Excel file with modifications. Requirements Python 3.x openpyxl library Installation bash Copy Edit pip install openpyxl Usage Ensure your Excel file (e.g., sales.xlsx) exists in the script's directory. Run the script with: python Copy Edit process_spreadsheet("sales.xlsx") The modified file is saved as sales_updated.xlsx.

title: AugustoNP/Historico-Faturamento_project:README.md
text: Repository for the billing history of **ISM GOMES DE MATTOS**, covering the years **2024** and **2025**. This is a **public version** of the README, with all sensitive data removed. All mentions of units or billing data are for educational and demonstrative purposes only. The billing includes: - All **UFC** RUs in Fortaleza - The **HGF** (Hospital Geral de Fortaleza) - The **IJF** (Instituto Doutor José Frota) - Several **UPAs** - The **UFPB** (Federal University of Paraíba) - The **UNB** (University of Brasília) - And approximately *33 more* units, mostly federal hospitals and universities. The project reads and consolidates multiple data tables into a standardized format, classifies them, and corrects inconsistencies. ---

title: IamRakibAhmed/linkedin-voucher-checker:readme.md
text: ![Python](https://img.shields.io/badge/Python-3.8%2B-blue?logo=python&logoColor=white) ![Selenium](https://img.shields.io/badge/Selenium-Automation-green?logo=selenium&logoColor=white) ![Pandas](https://img.shields.io/badge/Pandas-Data_Analysis-blue?logo=pandas&logoColor=white) ![OpenPyXL](https://img.shields.io/badge/OpenPyXL-Excel_Integration-green) A Python Selenium-based project designed to automate the process of validating LinkedIn URLs. The script logs into LinkedIn, processes each URL from a given list, checks their status, and handles errors gracefully while maintaining a clean, professional code structure. ---

title: IamRakibAhmed/linkedin-voucher-checker:readme.md
text: - **Automated Login**: Logs into LinkedIn using your credentials. - **URL Validation**: - Detects "Offer unavailable" messages. - Halts execution on "Something went wrong" and updates the URL list accordingly. - **Excel Integration**: Dynamically updates the input Excel file to retain only unprocessed URLs. - **Logs**: - Detailed logging for each step of the process. - Logs stored in a dedicated log file for easy debugging. - **Error Handling**: Graceful termination and updates on encountering issues. ---

title: IamRakibAhmed/linkedin-voucher-checker:readme.md
text: - ![Python](https://img.shields.io/badge/Python-3.8%2B-blue?logo=python&logoColor=white) Python 3.8+ - ![Selenium](https://img.shields.io/badge/Selenium-Automation-green?logo=selenium&logoColor=white) Selenium - ![Pandas](https://img.shields.io/badge/Pandas-Data_Analysis-blue?logo=pandas&logoColor=white) Pandas - ![OpenPyXL](https://img.shields.io/badge/OpenPyXL-Excel_Integration-green) OpenPyXL Install the required libraries using: ---

title: IamRakibAhmed/linkedin-voucher-checker:readme.md
text: Place your `urls.xlsx` file in the project root. Ensure it contains a column named `URLs` with the LinkedIn links to validate.

title: IamRakibAhmed/linkedin-voucher-checker:readme.md
text: - Logs are stored in `logs/linkedin_checker.log`. - The log file contains detailed information about the execution, including: - Login status. - Status of each URL processed (`Valid`, `Skipped`, or `Unchecked`). - Errors and exceptions, if any. ---

title: IamRakibAhmed/linkedin-voucher-checker:readme.md
text: 1. **Updated URLs File**: - The script updates `urls.xlsx` to retain only the unprocessed URLs if "Something went wrong" is encountered. 2. **Valid URLs**: - Valid URLs are saved in a new Excel file named `valid_urls.xlsx`. ---

title: IamRakibAhmed/linkedin-voucher-checker:readme.md
text: - **"Something went wrong"**: - Logs the error and updates `urls.xlsx` to exclude already-processed URLs. - Halts further processing. - **Other Errors**: - Logs exceptions with the corresponding URL for debugging. ---
